content.problemStatement:
While vacationing in Egypt, Chef found a recipe of an ancient dish. The receipt is a sequence of hieroglyphs written on a magic paper - it survived for thousands of years! The sequence is written on a long scroll. Unfortunately, the scroll is split into pieces, and possibly, some pieces of the scroll are missing. Chef has a total of **N** pieces, numbered for convenience from **1** to **N**. He gave the pieces of a tape to his friend who studies hieroglyphs for analysis. Chef was happy to hear that there are some relations between the pieces. Some of the pieces may follow other pieces.

The informations about these relations is given as a list of **M** pairs (**Ai**, **Bi**). Such a pair means that the **Ai**th piece can be immediately followed by the **Bi**th one.

Chef is working on recovering the recipe to prepare this dish for his Egyptian friends. He wants to place all the pieces into lines such that any pair of consecutive pieces (**L**, **R**) in a line must be explicitly allowed by one of the **M** rules described above. In other words, for some **i** (1 ≤ **i** ≤ **M**), (**Ai**, **Bi**) = (**L**, **R**). It appears that in general, not all the pieces can be placed in a single line while following the rules. So, please help Chef find what is the minimum number of lines required for all the pieces to be placed acoording to the rules.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and M – the number of pieces and the number of allowed consecutive relations. Then M lines follow, each containing two integers A and B (1‑based) meaning piece A can be immediately followed by piece B. It is guaranteed that the directed graph formed by the M pairs has no directed cycle.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the minimum number of lines required to place all N pieces respecting the given relations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: edges form a chain 1→2→3, so one line suffices.\nTest case 2: piece 1 can go to 2 and also to 3, but 2 and 3 cannot be connected, thus we need two lines: 1→2 and 1→3.\nTest case 3: optimal cover is 1→3→4 and 2→3→4→5, requiring two lines.
First test case: edges form a total order 1→2→3→4, so a single line covers all pieces.\nSecond test case: we have two independent edges 1→2 and 3→4, therefore two separate lines are needed.
Maximum matching size is 3 (1→2,2→3,4→5). Hence answer = 5‑3 = 2 lines.

solution.approach: The problem asks for the minimum number of vertex‑disjoint directed paths that together cover all vertices of a DAG. For a DAG this value equals N minus the size of a maximum matching in the bipartite graph constructed from the DAG: split each vertex v into a left copy v_L and a right copy v_R; for every directed edge u→v in the original graph add an edge from u_L to v_R. A matching chooses at most one outgoing edge for each vertex and at most one incoming edge for each vertex, exactly what a path cover needs. Hence answer = N – maxMatching. We compute the maximum matching with the classic Kuhn (DFS augmenting path) algorithm, which runs in O(N·M) – easily fast for N ≤ 100. Steps per test case: 1) build adjacency list from left side to right side; 2) run Kuhn to obtain maximum matching size; 3) output N – matchingSize.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M) per test case (Kuhn algorithm). With N ≤ 100 this is well within limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) for adjacency lists and matching arrays.
